guidelines.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines: Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, and Pancreatic (v2.2025)
guidelines.excerpt: NCCN recommends multi-gene panel testing for individuals who meet testing criteria based on personal and/or family history. Testing criteria include: breast cancer diagnosed at any age with a close relative with breast cancer ≤50, ovarian cancer, male breast cancer, or pancreatic cancer; breast cancer ≤45 years; triple-negative breast cancer ≤60 years; two or more primary breast cancers with first diagnosed ≤50; ovarian cancer at any age; male breast cancer at any age; pancreatic cancer with ≥1 close relative with breast, ovarian, or pancreatic cancer; prostate cancer (metastatic, high-risk group, very-high-risk group, or Ashkenazi Jewish ancestry); ≥3 breast cancer diagnoses in patient and/or close blood relatives on same side of family; known pathogenic/likely pathogenic variant in a cancer susceptibility gene in the family (cascade testing); individuals with ≥5% probability of BRCA1/2 mutation on validated prediction model. Multi-gene panel testing should include genes with established clinical actionability, including BRCA1, BRCA2, PALB2, ATM, CHEK2, TP53, PTEN, CDH1, STK11, and others as clinically indicated.
keyPoints: Multi-gene panel testing recommended for all qualifying individuals
Breast cancer ≤45 years qualifies regardless of family history
Triple-negative breast cancer ≤60 years qualifies
Multiple primary cancers in patient or family is an indication
Ovarian cancer at ANY age qualifies for testing
Known familial pathogenic variant warrants cascade testing
Prostate cancer meeting risk criteria (metastatic, high/very-high risk, or Ashkenazi) qualifies
Pancreatic cancer with family history of breast/ovarian/pancreatic qualifies
Panel should include BRCA1/2, PALB2, ATM, CHEK2, TP53, PTEN, CDH1, STK11
Testing results impact surgical decisions, PARP inhibitor eligibility, and cascade testing

guidelines.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines: Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Colorectal, Endometrial, and Related (v1.2025)
guidelines.excerpt: Multi-gene panel testing is recommended for individuals with a personal history of colorectal cancer diagnosed ≤50 years, endometrial cancer diagnosed ≤60 years, or other Lynch syndrome-associated cancers with MSI-H/dMMR tumor testing results. Testing is also recommended for individuals meeting revised Bethesda guidelines, Amsterdam II criteria, or with ≥2 first-degree relatives with Lynch-associated cancers. For prostate cancer, testing is recommended for patients with a significant family history of Lynch-associated cancers or with MSI-H tumors. Results inform cancer surveillance intensity, interval, and modality; risk-reducing surgery decisions (prophylactic hysterectomy/oophorectomy, colectomy); immunotherapy eligibility for MSI-H tumors; and family cascade testing for identified variants.
keyPoints: Colorectal cancer ≤50 years qualifies for germline testing
Endometrial cancer ≤60 years qualifies for germline testing
MSI-H or dMMR tumor testing results trigger germline testing
Revised Bethesda guidelines and Amsterdam II criteria apply
Family history of ≥2 first-degree relatives with Lynch cancers supports testing
Results guide surveillance frequency and risk-reducing surgery
Immunotherapy eligibility for MSI-H tumors informed by testing
Prostate cancer patients with Lynch family history should be tested
Cascade testing recommended for all first-degree relatives of variant carriers
Panel should include MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, EPCAM at minimum

guidelines.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines: Prostate Cancer — Germline Testing Recommendations (v4.2025)
guidelines.excerpt: NCCN recommends germline testing for all men with metastatic prostate cancer regardless of family history. For localized prostate cancer, germline testing is recommended for: high-risk or very-high-risk group; Ashkenazi Jewish ancestry; known family pathogenic variant; strong family history (≥1 close relative with breast cancer ≤50, ovarian cancer, pancreatic cancer, or metastatic/high-grade prostate cancer). Actionable genes include BRCA2 (most impactful for prostate — associated with higher grade, earlier onset, and PARP inhibitor eligibility), BRCA1, ATM, PALB2, CHEK2, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, and HOXB13. BRCA2 carriers with prostate cancer may be eligible for olaparib or other PARP inhibitors.
keyPoints: ALL metastatic prostate cancer patients should undergo germline testing
High-risk and very-high-risk localized prostate cancer qualifies
Ashkenazi Jewish ancestry with prostate cancer qualifies
BRCA2 is the most impactful gene — associated with aggressive disease
PARP inhibitor eligibility (olaparib) for BRCA1/2 carriers
Family history of breast ≤50, ovarian, or pancreatic cancer supports testing
ATM, PALB2, CHEK2 variants affect cancer management
HOXB13 mutations associated with hereditary prostate cancer
